codeReview.summary: The feature adds My Tickets and My Listings navigation links to the account center (/my/page.tsx) and creates comprehensive test suites for both existing pages. The implementation is well-scoped: the worker correctly identified that both My Tickets (/my/tickets) and My Listings (/my/listings) pages already existed with full data fetching, status badges, ordering, and trust messaging from prior milestones. The commit adds 2 navigation links with appropriate icons (Ticket, Tag) and repositions existing menu items with updated colors. Both test files (362 and 400 lines) provide thorough coverage of loading states, empty states, populated data rendering, status badge display, ordering verification, item type display, multi-status coverage, and Buyer Protection messaging. API routes correctly scope data to the authenticated user (via session email lookup) and order results chronologically (newest first). The middleware properly protects /my/** routes from unauthenticated access. Seed data provides 5 owned assets and 5 listings across multiple lifecycle statuses for the test user.

issues.description: Error handling for failed fetch only logs to console.error and silently shows empty state. The user sees 'No tickets yet' even on network/server errors, which could be confusing. Consider showing an error state to distinguish between 'no data' and 'failed to load'.
Same silent error handling pattern as My Tickets - failed fetches show empty state instead of an error message. This applies to both pages identically.

The Buyer Protection test assertion uses a loose check (document.body.textContent?.includes or querySelector with class*=). While functional, this is fragile - if the BuyerProtection component changes its text or class naming, the test may silently pass via the fallback OR path rather than testing what it claims to test.
Same fragile Buyer Protection assertion pattern as the tickets test. Both tests use an OR-chain (querySelector || textContent.includes) that could mask test intent.
No test covers the error/failure state when the API returns a non-ok response. Both test files only test the happy path (loading, empty, populated) and skip the fetch failure path.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Both My Tickets and My Listings pages use identical error handling patterns: console.error on fetch failure and displaying the empty state instead of a distinct error UI. This is consistent with other pages in the app but could be documented as a convention or flagged as a known gap.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: app/my/tickets/page.tsx:38-44 and app/my/listings/page.tsx:38-44 both catch errors and only console.error, showing empty state to user.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed the marketplace-worker skill procedure correctly: read feature/context, checked existing code, ran baseline tests, implemented changes, ran validators (vitest, typecheck, lint), and verified with agent-browser. Skill feedback confirms followedProcedure=true with no deviations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows proper sequence: skill invocation -> context reading -> baseline validation -> implementation -> test creation -> all validators pass -> agent-browser verification.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Test files are placed in __tests__/components/my/ matching the app/my/ page structure. This follows the existing test organization convention seen in other test files.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: __tests__/components/my/MyTicketsPage.test.tsx and __tests__/components/my/MyListingsPage.test.tsx follow the same pattern as other existing test files.

summary: Pass. The feature correctly adds My Tickets and My Listings navigation to the account center and provides comprehensive test coverage for both pages. The pages themselves (with full data fetching, status badges, ordering, user-scoping, and trust messaging) already existed from prior milestones. The commit is minimal and focused: 2 new nav links with icons and 2 thorough test files (19 total test cases). All validators pass. Non-blocking issues include silent error handling (empty state shown on fetch failure instead of error state) and slightly fragile Buyer Protection test assertions, but these do not block the feature's correctness or the validation contract requirements.
